summary:
Panini's Grammar (Ashtadhyayi, c. 400 BC) is the most comprehensive and systematic grammatical description of any ancient language, producing a complete account of Sanskrit morphology and syntax in 3,959 sutras (aphorisms) that is so precise and rigorous that it has been compared to modern formal linguistics — and was composed in a pre-computational age using an entirely mental, memorized format.

Panini, who lived near [[event:1109|University of Takshashila]], wrote the Ashtadhyayi ('Eight Chapters') as a complete generative grammar of Sanskrit — the sacred language of the Vedic tradition. The text uses a meta-language of symbols and rules to describe how Sanskrit words are formed and combined, anticipating formal linguistic theory by over two millennia. The Ashtadhyayi is so complete that it successfully describes virtually all of Sanskrit as then known, with a brevity that makes it a compressed encoding of an entire linguistic system. When Western linguists began studying Sanskrit seriously in the late 18th and early 19th centuries, Panini's achievement stunned them — it contributed to the development of historical linguistics and the concept of Proto-Indo-European. Modern computational linguists regard Panini's grammar as a precursor to formal language theory.
